text: The laparoscopic view shows the hepatic flexure of the colon in close proximity to the gallbladder. The surgeon clears surrounding tissue at an accelerated speed, revealing no distinct separation plane between the colon and gallbladder.

The surgeon attempts a top-down dissection but finds the colon adhesed to the liver with no separation from the gallbladder. The gallbladder is transected at a midpoint between the colon and portal structures, allowing safe dissection from the liver fossa despite not encountering the gallbladder lumen.

The surgeon transects the gallbladder a second time, revealing thickened walls and a narrow lumen filled with biliary sludge. Further dissection exposes a narrowing consistent with the transition from the gallbladder to the cystic duct. A nick is made in the cystic duct, and a balloon tip cholangiogram catheter is inserted. The cholangiogram displays the catheter in a short cystic duct with the balloon tip positioned in the common bile duct, clearly showing the left and right hepatic ducts and contrast entering the duodenum.

The surgeon focuses on the gallbladder fundus and decides to remove a small segment of the colon using an endogia stapler, placed close to the liver to avoid narrowing the colon lumen. The remaining colon and gallbladder fundus are dissected free from the liver. An ultrasonic device aids in the dissection, revealing a large gallstone, which is set aside for later removal.

The extracted specimen shows the colon and gallbladder adhered together with a visible fistula tract between them. The surgeon secures the large cystic duct with a ten millimeter polymer clip applied through the umbilical extraction site, then resects the remaining cystic duct and gallbladder infundibulum to complete the procedure.
